questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is found unresponsive with pinpoint pupils, a respiratory rate of 7 breaths/min, and fresh injection sites on his arm. His ABG shows: pH 7.25, PaCO₂ 60 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 26 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Metabolic acidosis from lactic acid accumulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute respiratory acidosis from opioid overdose

explanation.summary: The clinical picture (pinpoint pupils, injection sites, low respiratory rate) indicates opioid overdose. The ABG shows acidemia (pH 7.25) with elevated PaCO₂ (60 mmHg) and normal HCO₃⁻ (26 mEq/L, minimal compensation), confirming acute respiratory acidosis from opioid-induced hypoventilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Opioids depress the brainstem respiratory centers, causing hypoventilation with decreased respiratory rate and tidal volume. The resulting CO₂ retention produces acute respiratory acidosis. The near-normal HCO₃⁻ confirms the acute nature — the kidneys have not had time to compensate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metabolic acidosis would show low HCO₃⁻ and low PaCO₂ (respiratory compensation). This patient has high PaCO₂ with normal HCO₃⁻, indicating a primary respiratory process.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic respiratory acidosis would show significantly elevated HCO₃⁻ (renal compensation over days) and a less severely reduced pH for the same PaCO₂. This patient's near-normal HCO₃⁻ indicates acute onset.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Respiratory alkalosis would show elevated pH with low PaCO₂. This patient has the opposite — low pH with high PaCO₂.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metabolic alkalosis would show elevated pH, elevated HCO₃⁻, and possibly elevated PaCO₂ from compensatory hypoventilation. This patient has acidemia with high PaCO₂ as the primary disturbance.

questions.stem: The two major mechanisms causing respiratory acidosis are impaired ventilation and impaired gas exchange. Which of the following is an example of impaired ventilation causing respiratory acidosis?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Opioid overdose depressing the respiratory center

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary edema thickening the alveolar membrane

explanation.summary: Opioid overdose impairs ventilation by depressing brainstem respiratory centers, leading to decreased respiratory rate and tidal volume. This reduces alveolar ventilation, causing CO₂ retention. Impaired gas exchange (the other mechanism) involves diseases affecting the alveolar membrane itself.
explanation.whyCorrect: Opioids act on brainstem μ-receptors, reducing respiratory drive. This decreases both the rate and depth of breathing, directly reducing alveolar ventilation and causing CO₂ accumulation without any primary lung parenchymal disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Emphysema impairs gas exchange by destroying alveolar surface area and capillary beds. The primary mechanism is at the alveolar level, not ventilatory drive.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pneumonia impairs gas exchange by filling alveolar spaces with exudate, preventing CO₂ from moving across the membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary edema impairs gas exchange by adding fluid to the alveolar membrane, increasing the diffusion barrier.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary fibrosis impairs gas exchange by thickening the alveolar membrane, reducing diffusion capacity.

questions.stem: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys compensate by increasing serum bicarbonate. The mechanism of renal compensation involves:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excreting H⁺ and regenerating new HCO₃⁻

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing CO₂ excretion via the kidneys

explanation.summary: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys compensate by increasing H⁺ excretion (buffered by phosphate and ammonium in the urine) and regenerating new bicarbonate to add to the blood. This raises serum HCO₃⁻, helping to restore the HCO₃⁻/CO₂ ratio toward normal and improving pH.
explanation.whyCorrect: For each H⁺ excreted by the kidneys, one new HCO₃⁻ is regenerated and added to the blood. This additional bicarbonate provides extra buffering capacity, partially restoring the Henderson-Hasselbalch ratio and raising pH toward normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Excreting HCO₃⁻ would worsen acidosis by removing buffering capacity. The kidneys retain and regenerate bicarbonate in respiratory acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The kidneys cannot excrete CO₂. CO₂ is a volatile acid eliminated exclusively by the lungs through ventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carbonic anhydrase activity in the kidneys actually increases during compensation, facilitating H⁺ secretion and HCO₃⁻ reabsorption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Volatile acids (CO₂) are produced by metabolism and eliminated by the lungs. Producing more would worsen acidosis.

questions.stem: A patient with acute respiratory acidosis from cardiac arrest has: pH 7.20, PaCO₂ 65 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 25 mEq/L. Compared to a patient with chronic respiratory acidosis at the same PaCO₂, how would the bicarbonate differ?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute has higher HCO₃⁻ than chronic

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic has higher HCO₃⁻ than acute

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both would have the same HCO₃⁻

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic would have lower HCO₃⁻ than acute

choices.label: E
choices.text: HCO₃⁻ does not change in respiratory acidosis

explanation.summary: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys have had days to weeks to compensate by increasing H⁺ excretion and bicarbonate regeneration. This produces significantly elevated serum HCO₃⁻ (often 30-40 mEq/L) compared to acute respiratory acidosis, where HCO₃⁻ rises only minimally (1-2 mEq/L).
explanation.whyCorrect: Renal compensation takes 3-5 days to reach full effect. In chronic disease, the kidneys have maximized H⁺ excretion and HCO₃⁻ generation, significantly raising serum bicarbonate. In acute disease, there is insufficient time for meaningful renal compensation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute respiratory acidosis has minimal time for renal compensation, so HCO₃⁻ remains near normal. Chronic disease has significantly higher HCO₃⁻ from sustained renal compensation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HCO₃⁻ differs significantly between acute and chronic respiratory acidosis at the same PaCO₂ due to the time-dependent renal compensation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic respiratory acidosis has higher, not lower, HCO₃⁻. The kidneys compensate by retaining and generating bicarbonate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HCO₃⁻ does change in respiratory acidosis. In chronic disease, renal compensation significantly increases serum bicarbonate.

questions.stem: A somnolent patient with suspected opioid overdose is brought to the emergency department. Before a complete workup, which medication should be administered immediately?

explanation.summary: Naloxone is an opioid antagonist that rapidly reverses opioid-induced respiratory depression. In suspected opioid overdose with hypoventilation, naloxone should be given immediately — even before a complete diagnostic workup — because it can be life-saving and is very safe.
explanation.whyCorrect: Naloxone competitively binds opioid receptors, reversing respiratory depression within minutes. It is the definitive treatment for the underlying cause of opioid-induced respiratory acidosis. Patients typically increase their respiratory rate and become more alert promptly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Albuterol is a bronchodilator for asthma and COPD. It does not reverse opioid-induced respiratory depression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sodium bicarbonate treats metabolic acidosis. In opioid overdose, the acidosis is respiratory (from CO₂ retention), and the treatment is to restore ventilation, not buffer the blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Epinephrine is used in cardiac arrest and anaphylaxis. It is not the first-line treatment for opioid overdose.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Furosemide is a diuretic used for fluid overload. It does not address opioid-induced hypoventilation.

questions.stem: The body produces over 15,000 millimoles of CO₂-derived acid per day. These are classified as volatile acids because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are produced in the liver

choices.label: B
choices.text: They can be eliminated as CO₂ gas through the lungs

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are excreted by the kidneys

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are buffered exclusively by proteins

choices.label: E
choices.text: They cannot be measured on blood gas analysis

explanation.summary: Volatile acids are acids that can be converted to a gas (CO₂) and eliminated through the lungs via ventilation. CO₂ from metabolism combines with H₂O to form H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid), which generates H⁺. By exhaling CO₂, the lungs remove this acid load. Non-volatile acids (fixed acids) must be excreted by the kidneys.
explanation.whyCorrect: The term 'volatile' means capable of being converted to vapor. CO₂-derived carbonic acid is volatile because the lungs can eliminate CO₂ through ventilation, effectively removing the acid. This distinguishes volatile acids from non-volatile (fixed) acids that require renal excretion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While metabolic CO₂ is produced throughout the body (including the liver via the Krebs cycle), the defining feature of volatile acids is their elimination via the lungs, not their site of production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Non-volatile (fixed) acids like sulfuric and phosphoric acid are excreted by the kidneys. Volatile acids are eliminated by the lungs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Multiple buffer systems (bicarbonate, hemoglobin, phosphate, proteins) handle volatile acids. Hemoglobin is a particularly important buffer for H⁺ from CO₂.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CO₂-derived acid is directly measured on arterial blood gas analysis as PaCO₂. It is one of the primary measured values.

questions.stem: Elevated CO₂ in the blood directly affects the brain in several ways. Which of the following neurological effects is caused by hypercapnia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cerebral vasoconstriction and decreased intracranial pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cerebral vasodilation and increased intracranial pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased CSF pH with no neurological effects

explanation.summary: High CO₂ levels cause cerebral vasodilation, which increases cerebral blood volume and intracranial pressure. Additionally, CO₂ diffuses into the CSF, lowering its pH, which alters sodium/calcium channels and neurotransmitter activity, contributing to altered mental status.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypercapnia causes cerebral vasodilation (CO₂ is a potent cerebral vasodilator), increasing intracranial pressure and decreasing cerebral perfusion pressure. The resulting low CSF pH disrupts neural function, causing symptoms ranging from confusion to coma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypercapnia causes vasodilation, not vasoconstriction. Cerebral vasoconstriction occurs with hypocapnia (low CO₂), as seen in hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: High CO₂ impairs neurotransmitter function by lowering CSF pH and altering ion channel activity. It does not improve brain function.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased CSF pH does have significant neurological effects, including altered mental status, confusion, and coma. It is not benign.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypercapnia affects the brain globally, not selectively the spinal cord. The primary effects are cerebral vasodilation and CSF acidification.

questions.stem: A patient with COPD exacerbation presents with respiratory acidosis. Initial treatment with bronchodilators does not improve the acidosis. Which of the following ventilatory supports should be initiated next?

explanation.summary: BiPAP provides higher pressure during inspiration (IPAP) and lower pressure during expiration (EPAP), effectively increasing tidal volume and helping 'blow off' CO₂. It is the preferred non-invasive ventilation for respiratory acidosis in COPD exacerbations that do not improve with bronchodilators.
explanation.whyCorrect: BiPAP augments ventilation by providing pressure support during inspiration, increasing tidal volume and alveolar ventilation. This directly addresses CO₂ retention by increasing the rate of CO₂ elimination. The expiratory pressure prevents alveolar collapse.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPAP provides a single continuous pressure that keeps airways open but does not actively augment tidal volume. It is less effective than BiPAP for improving ventilation and CO₂ clearance in respiratory acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: High-flow nasal cannula delivers humidified oxygen at high flow rates and provides some positive pressure, but it does not provide the inspiratory pressure support needed to significantly augment ventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Incentive spirometry encourages deep breathing post-operatively to prevent atelectasis. It does not provide the ventilatory support needed in acute respiratory acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chest physiotherapy helps clear secretions but does not directly augment ventilation or address CO₂ retention in acute respiratory acidosis.

questions.stem: Which of the following neuromuscular diseases can cause respiratory acidosis by impairing respiratory muscle function?

explanation.summary: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease that attacks acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, causing skeletal muscle weakness including the respiratory muscles. Weakness of the diaphragm and intercostals reduces tidal volume, leading to hypoventilation and respiratory acidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Myasthenia gravis impairs neuromuscular transmission at the diaphragm and intercostal muscles, directly reducing the mechanical ability to generate adequate tidal volume. This decreased ventilation causes CO₂ retention and respiratory acidosis, especially during myasthenic crisis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Parkinson disease primarily affects the basal ganglia, causing motor symptoms like tremor and rigidity. While severe cases may have respiratory complications, it is not a classic cause of respiratory acidosis from muscle weakness.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Multiple sclerosis is a CNS demyelinating disease. While respiratory involvement can occur in advanced cases, it is not a primary neuromuscular cause of respiratory acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alzheimer disease is a neurodegenerative dementia. It does not directly cause respiratory muscle weakness or respiratory acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Essential tremor is a movement disorder causing involuntary shaking. It does not affect respiratory muscle function.

questions.stem: The Krebs cycle produces over 15,000 mmol of CO₂ per day. The body's primary defense against acidification from this CO₂ production is:

explanation.summary: CO₂ is a volatile acid that is eliminated primarily through pulmonary ventilation. The lungs exhale CO₂ continuously, matching its production rate under normal conditions to maintain PaCO₂ at ~40 mmHg and prevent acidification.
explanation.whyCorrect: The lungs are the primary organ for eliminating CO₂-derived volatile acid. Normal ventilation removes CO₂ at the same rate it is produced, maintaining acid-base homeostasis. When ventilation fails, CO₂ accumulates and respiratory acidosis develops.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The kidneys cannot excrete CO₂ (a volatile acid). They handle non-volatile (fixed) acids and regulate bicarbonate. CO₂ must be eliminated by the lungs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The liver produces CO₂ through metabolism but does not eliminate it. CO₂ removal requires the lungs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Protein buffers (including hemoglobin) temporarily neutralize H⁺ from CO₂, but the primary defense is CO₂ elimination by the lungs. Buffering alone cannot handle 15,000 mmol/day.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sweat glands excrete small amounts of acid but play a negligible role in CO₂ or acid-base management.

questions.stem: A patient presents with acute respiratory acidosis. The ABG shows: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 58 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 27 mEq/L. Which findings would you expect if this same patient had chronic respiratory acidosis at the same PaCO₂?

choices.label: A
choices.text: pH closer to normal, HCO₃⁻ significantly elevated (e.g., 35 mEq/L)

choices.label: E
choices.text: pH and HCO₃⁻ both return completely to normal

explanation.summary: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys have had time (3-5 days) to maximally compensate by retaining bicarbonate. At the same PaCO₂ of 58, the HCO₃⁻ would be significantly elevated (e.g., 35 mEq/L) and the pH would be closer to normal (though still below 7.35).
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic renal compensation raises HCO₃⁻ by approximately 3.5 mEq/L for each 10 mmHg rise in PaCO₂ above 40. For PaCO₂ of 58: expected HCO₃⁻ ≈ 24 + (3.5 × 1.8) ≈ 30-35 mEq/L. This brings pH closer to normal without fully correcting it.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic disease has a less acidic pH than acute disease at the same PaCO₂ because of renal compensation. HCO₃⁻ is increased, not decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.C: pH and HCO₃⁻ are significantly different between acute and chronic respiratory acidosis at the same PaCO₂. Renal compensation makes a measurable difference.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Compensation does not overshoot to produce alkalosis. pH improves toward normal but does not become alkalotic. HCO₃⁻ increases, not decreases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Compensation improves pH toward normal but does not fully correct it to 7.40. Complete correction would require fixing the underlying respiratory problem.

questions.stem: A patient with Guillain-Barré syndrome develops progressive ascending paralysis affecting the respiratory muscles. Which of the following ABG patterns would be expected?

explanation.summary: Guillain-Barré syndrome causes ascending demyelinating polyneuropathy that can paralyze respiratory muscles (diaphragm, intercostals). This reduces tidal volume and causes acute hypoventilation with CO₂ retention — producing acute respiratory acidosis (low pH, high PaCO₂, normal HCO₃⁻).
explanation.whyCorrect: Respiratory muscle weakness from GBS reduces ventilation, causing CO₂ accumulation. The ABG shows acidemia (pH 7.25) with elevated PaCO₂ (60 mmHg) and near-normal HCO₃⁻ (25 mEq/L), consistent with acute respiratory acidosis without renal compensation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This ABG shows respiratory alkalosis (high pH, low PaCO₂), which indicates hyperventilation. GBS causes hypoventilation, not hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This ABG shows metabolic acidosis (low pH, normal PaCO₂, low HCO₃⁻). GBS primarily causes respiratory acidosis from muscle weakness.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This ABG shows metabolic alkalosis (high pH, normal PaCO₂, high HCO₃⁻). This is not caused by respiratory muscle weakness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This ABG is completely normal. A patient with respiratory muscle weakness significant enough to cause symptoms would have abnormal gas values.

questions.stem: Respiratory acidosis results when alveolar ventilation is inadequate relative to CO₂ production. The normal pH range that the body maintains is:

explanation.summary: The body tightly regulates blood pH within the narrow range of 7.35-7.45. Below 7.35 is acidemia; above 7.45 is alkalemia. Deviations outside this range impair protein function and cellular processes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normal blood pH is 7.35-7.45. This narrow range is essential for proper enzyme function, protein structure, and cellular processes. The lungs and kidneys work together to maintain pH within this range.

whyWrongByChoice.A: pH of 6.80-7.00 represents severe, life-threatening acidemia. Normal pH is much higher.
whyWrongByChoice.B: pH of 7.00-7.20 represents significant acidemia that impairs cellular function. Normal range starts at 7.35.
whyWrongByChoice.D: pH of 7.50-7.60 represents alkalemia. While not as immediately dangerous as severe acidemia, this is above the normal range.
whyWrongByChoice.E: pH of 7.80-8.00 represents severe alkalemia incompatible with life. Normal pH is far below this.

questions.stem: A patient with obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) presents with chronic respiratory acidosis. Which of the following mechanisms best explains how obesity causes hypoventilation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess adipose tissue restricts diaphragm excursion and chest wall expansion

explanation.summary: In obesity hypoventilation syndrome, excessive adipose tissue in the abdomen and chest wall restricts diaphragm movement and limits thoracic expansion. This mechanical limitation reduces tidal volume and alveolar ventilation, leading to chronic CO₂ retention and respiratory acidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The weight of excess adipose tissue on the chest wall and abdomen impedes diaphragmatic descent during inspiration, reducing tidal volume. This mechanical restriction, combined with potential central sleep apnea, leads to chronic hypoventilation with CO₂ retention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Obesity does not increase surfactant production. Surfactant is unrelated to the mechanism of obesity-induced hypoventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OHS is not caused by pulmonary fibrosis. The mechanism is mechanical restriction of ventilation by excess adipose tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adipose tissue does not absorb CO₂. CO₂ is eliminated through the lungs via ventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Obesity does not increase alveolar surface area. The problem in OHS is restricted chest wall mechanics, not alveolar changes.

questions.stem: A patient with markedly decreased PaO₂ and rising PaCO₂ despite BiPAP requires the next level of respiratory support. Which intervention is indicated?

explanation.summary: When non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) fails to improve oxygenation and CO₂ clearance, or the patient shows signs of impending respiratory arrest (progressive fatigue, worsening ABG), endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation are required to completely take over the work of breathing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endotracheal intubation with mechanical ventilation provides definitive airway control and fully supports ventilation. It is indicated when non-invasive measures fail, as evidenced by worsening hypoxemia, rising PaCO₂, and clinical deterioration despite BiPAP.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen alone does not address the ventilatory failure (rising PaCO₂). The patient needs ventilatory support, not just oxygen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhaled corticosteroids treat airway inflammation (asthma, COPD maintenance) but do not address acute ventilatory failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oral antibiotics may be appropriate if infection is the underlying cause, but they do not provide the urgent ventilatory support needed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chest physiotherapy helps clear secretions but cannot address progressive respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation.

questions.stem: Which of the following ABG patterns is consistent with respiratory acidosis?

explanation.summary: Respiratory acidosis shows: decreased pH (acidemia), elevated PaCO₂ (the primary disturbance from CO₂ retention), and elevated HCO₃⁻ (renal compensation, more pronounced in chronic disease). In acute cases, HCO₃⁻ may be normal or only slightly elevated.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hallmark of respiratory acidosis is elevated PaCO₂ from inadequate ventilation. This drives pH down. The kidneys compensate by retaining bicarbonate, raising HCO₃⁻, especially in chronic disease. The pattern is: pH ↓, PaCO₂ ↑, HCO₃⁻ ↑.

whyWrongByChoice.B: pH ↑ with PaCO₂ ↓ describes respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation), the opposite of respiratory acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: pH ↓ with PaCO₂ ↓ and HCO₃⁻ ↓ describes metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (hyperventilation). The primary disturbance is low HCO₃⁻.
whyWrongByChoice.D: pH ↑ with PaCO₂ ↑ and HCO₃⁻ ↓ is not a consistent physiologic pattern. Elevated PaCO₂ would decrease pH, not increase it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: pH ↑ with both PaCO₂ ↑ and HCO₃⁻ ↑ could suggest metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation, not respiratory acidosis.

questions.stem: Central sleep apnea causes respiratory acidosis through which mechanism?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure of the brainstem to signal respiratory muscles during sleep

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alveolar destruction reducing gas exchange surface

explanation.summary: Central sleep apnea (CSA) results from failure of the brainstem respiratory centers to send appropriate signals to the respiratory muscles during sleep. This leads to periodic cessation of breathing, CO₂ retention, and respiratory acidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In CSA, the CNS fails to generate the neural impulse to breathe, unlike obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) where the airway is physically blocked. The absence of respiratory effort during apneic episodes causes hypoventilation and CO₂ accumulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pharyngeal collapse during sleep describes obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), not central sleep apnea. In CSA, the airway is open but respiratory effort is absent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alveolar destruction characterizes emphysema. CSA is a disorder of respiratory drive, not lung parenchymal disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surfactant deficiency causes neonatal RDS. It is not the mechanism of central sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchospasm occurs in asthma and reactive airway disease. CSA is caused by CNS failure to signal breathing, not airway inflammation.

questions.stem: Each 0.3 change in pH corresponds to what change in hydrogen ion concentration?

explanation.summary: The relationship between pH and [H⁺] is logarithmic. Each 0.3 unit decrease in pH corresponds to a doubling of [H⁺], and each 0.3 unit increase corresponds to a halving. This means small pH changes represent significant shifts in hydrogen ion concentration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Since pH = -log[H⁺], a 0.3 change in pH corresponds to a 2-fold (100%) change in [H⁺]. For example, a pH drop from 7.40 to 7.10 means [H⁺] has doubled. This underscores why the body tightly regulates pH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A 10% change would correspond to a much smaller pH shift than 0.3 units. The logarithmic scale means 0.3 units equals a 2-fold change.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A 25% change is less than the actual 100% (doubling) that corresponds to a 0.3 pH change.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A 100-fold change would correspond to a full 2-unit pH change (e.g., from 7.4 to 5.4), not 0.3 units.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A 0.3 pH change represents a significant doubling or halving of H⁺ concentration. It is clinically important.

questions.stem: Which of the following diagnostic tests is most helpful for initially confirming the diagnosis of respiratory acidosis?

explanation.summary: Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis directly measures pH, PaCO₂, PaO₂, and calculates HCO₃⁻. This is the definitive test for diagnosing respiratory acidosis (low pH with elevated PaCO₂). Venous blood gas can also provide useful initial information.
explanation.whyCorrect: An ABG (or VBG) provides the direct measurements needed to confirm respiratory acidosis: decreased pH and elevated PaCO₂. Additional values (HCO₃⁻, base excess) help determine whether the acidosis is acute or chronic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest X-ray may help identify the underlying cause (pneumonia, COPD) but cannot confirm the acid-base diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary function testing evaluates lung mechanics (FEV₁, FVC) and can identify underlying diseases like COPD, but cannot diagnose acute acid-base disturbances.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulse oximetry measures oxygen saturation but not PaCO₂ or pH. Patients can have normal SpO₂ with significant hypercapnia and acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A complete blood count provides information about hemoglobin and white blood cells but does not measure acid-base parameters.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic respiratory acidosis from long-standing COPD is generally less symptomatic than a patient with acute respiratory acidosis at the same PaCO₂. This is primarily because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic patients have adapted to use anaerobic metabolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Renal compensation has partially restored pH toward normal

choices.label: C
choices.text: The respiratory centers become more sensitive to CO₂ over time

choices.label: E
choices.text: The lungs develop new alveoli to compensate

explanation.summary: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys have had time to compensate by retaining bicarbonate and excreting H⁺, partially restoring the blood pH toward normal. This improved pH reduces the severity of CNS and cardiac symptoms compared to acute respiratory acidosis at the same PaCO₂.
explanation.whyCorrect: Renal compensation raises serum bicarbonate over days to weeks, improving the HCO₃⁻/CO₂ ratio in the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. This partially restores pH, reducing the impact of elevated CO₂ on brain function and making chronic patients less symptomatic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chronic patients do not switch to anaerobic metabolism. Their reduced symptoms are from improved pH via renal bicarbonate retention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Respiratory centers actually become less sensitive to CO₂ in chronic hypercapnia, which is part of the pathophysiology — not a beneficial adaptation that reduces symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic hypercapnia does not eliminate all symptoms. Patients still have reduced exercise tolerance and may decompensate with additional stressors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The lungs do not regenerate new alveoli to compensate for chronic disease. Alveolar destruction in COPD is irreversible.

questions.stem: The bicarbonate buffer system is represented by the equation: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. In respiratory acidosis, the equilibrium shifts:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neither direction — the equilibrium is unaffected

explanation.summary: In respiratory acidosis, elevated CO₂ drives the equilibrium to the right: more CO₂ combines with H₂O to form H₂CO₃, which dissociates into H⁺ and HCO₃⁻. The increased H⁺ concentration lowers blood pH.
explanation.whyCorrect: By Le Chatelier's principle, increased CO₂ (reactant) shifts the equilibrium rightward, producing more carbonic acid, more H⁺, and more HCO₃⁻. The increased H⁺ is the direct cause of the acidemia in respiratory acidosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A leftward shift would decrease H⁺ and cause alkalosis. This occurs in respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation), not acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated CO₂ directly drives the equilibrium rightward per Le Chatelier's principle. The equilibrium is clearly affected.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A leftward shift would consume H⁺, not increase it. Increased CO₂ shifts the reaction rightward.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A rightward shift produces more H⁺, not less. Increased H⁺ is the hallmark of acidosis.

questions.stem: A patient with a suspected COPD exacerbation presents with cough, dyspnea, and the following ABG: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 62 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 30 mEq/L. The elevated bicarbonate suggests this patient has:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic respiratory acidosis with an acute exacerbation

explanation.summary: The pH is low (acidemia) with elevated PaCO₂ (respiratory acidosis). The HCO₃⁻ of 30 mEq/L is elevated above normal (22-28), indicating partial renal compensation — suggesting an underlying chronic respiratory acidosis. The significantly low pH despite compensation suggests an acute exacerbation on top of chronic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The elevated HCO₃⁻ indicates prior renal compensation, meaning the patient has had chronic CO₂ retention. However, the significantly reduced pH (7.28) suggests that PaCO₂ has risen acutely beyond what the current compensation can handle — an acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis typical of COPD exacerbation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In purely acute respiratory acidosis, HCO₃⁻ would be normal or only minimally elevated (1-2 mEq/L). The HCO₃⁻ of 30 indicates prior chronic compensation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Metabolic alkalosis would show elevated pH with elevated HCO₃⁻. This patient has acidemia with elevated PaCO₂ as the primary disturbance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Respiratory alkalosis would show elevated pH with decreased PaCO₂. This patient has the opposite — low pH with high PaCO₂.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The ABG shows clear abnormalities: low pH, high PaCO₂, and elevated HCO₃⁻. This is not a normal acid-base status.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden shortness of breath, palpitations, and perioral tingling after a stressful meeting. His respiratory rate is 32 breaths/min and SpO₂ is 99%. ABG shows: pH 7.52, PaCO₂ 28 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 23 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: The elevated pH (7.52) with decreased PaCO₂ (28 mmHg) confirms respiratory alkalosis. The normal HCO₃⁻ indicates an acute process without renal compensation. The clinical scenario (anxiety, stressful trigger, normal SpO₂) points to anxiety-driven hyperventilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute respiratory alkalosis is characterized by elevated pH, decreased PaCO₂, and normal HCO₃⁻ (no time for renal compensation). The clinical presentation — anxiety, rapid breathing, perioral tingling, and normal oxygenation — is classic for panic-induced hyperventilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Respiratory acidosis would show decreased pH with elevated PaCO₂. This patient has the opposite pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Metabolic alkalosis would show elevated pH with elevated HCO₃⁻ as the primary disturbance. This patient's HCO₃⁻ is normal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic respiratory alkalosis would show decreased HCO₃⁻ from renal compensation. This patient's HCO₃⁻ is normal, indicating acute onset.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation would show low pH, low HCO₃⁻, and low PaCO₂. This patient has elevated pH.

questions.stem: A patient with respiratory alkalosis develops tingling in the fingers and around the mouth. These paresthesias are caused by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased ionized calcium due to increased albumin binding in alkalemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct CO₂ effect on peripheral nerves

explanation.summary: In alkalemia, the increased pH causes calcium to shift from its ionized (active) form to albumin-bound form, decreasing free ionized calcium. This reduction in ionized calcium increases neuromuscular excitability, causing paresthesias, tingling, and potentially tetany.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alkalemia increases the negative charges on albumin, causing more calcium to bind to albumin. The resulting decrease in ionized (free) calcium promotes neuromuscular excitability by lowering the threshold for nerve firing, producing perioral and extremity paresthesias.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alkalemia causes hypokalemia (potassium shifts into cells), not hyperkalemia. While hypokalemia can cause symptoms, the paresthesias in respiratory alkalosis are primarily from decreased ionized calcium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CO₂ itself does not directly cause peripheral nerve tingling. The mechanism is the pH-dependent shift in calcium binding to albumin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypoglycemia is not a feature of respiratory alkalosis. The paresthesias are from decreased ionized calcium, not blood glucose changes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypernatremia is not caused by respiratory alkalosis. Sodium homeostasis is not primarily affected by acute changes in ventilation.

questions.stem: In chronic respiratory alkalosis, the kidneys compensate by:

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increasing carbonic anhydrase activity in the lungs

explanation.summary: In chronic respiratory alkalosis, the kidneys compensate for the elevated pH by excreting bicarbonate (reducing the blood's buffering capacity) and retaining H⁺. This lowers the HCO₃⁻/CO₂ ratio, bringing pH back toward normal.
explanation.whyCorrect: The kidneys respond to chronic alkalemia by reducing bicarbonate reabsorption and increasing bicarbonate excretion in the urine. This takes hours to days to reach full effect and lowers serum HCO₃⁻, partially correcting the elevated pH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Retaining bicarbonate and excreting H⁺ is the renal compensation for respiratory acidosis, not alkalosis. In alkalosis, the kidneys do the opposite.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The kidneys cannot increase CO₂ production as a compensation mechanism. CO₂ is produced by cellular metabolism throughout the body.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Volatile acid (CO₂) production is a metabolic process, not a renal compensatory mechanism. The kidneys handle non-volatile acids and bicarbonate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carbonic anhydrase in the lungs is not the mechanism of renal compensation. The kidneys alter bicarbonate handling to compensate.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman in the first trimester of pregnancy has an ABG showing: pH 7.44, PaCO₂ 32 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 21 mEq/L. The most likely explanation for these findings is:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progesterone-mediated increase in tidal volume causing chronic respiratory alkalosis

explanation.summary: Pregnancy increases progesterone levels, which stimulates the respiratory center and causes increased tidal volume. This physiologic hyperventilation produces a mild chronic respiratory alkalosis with low PaCO₂ and compensatory decrease in HCO₃⁻. The near-normal pH reflects renal compensation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Progesterone acts as a respiratory stimulant, increasing tidal volume and minute ventilation during pregnancy. The chronic mild hyperventilation lowers PaCO₂ to ~30-32 mmHg. Renal compensation (bicarbonate excretion) brings HCO₃⁻ down, maintaining pH near normal. This is a normal pregnancy finding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While anxiety can cause hyperventilation, the chronic nature (decreased HCO₃⁻ indicating compensation) and pregnancy context make progesterone-mediated changes more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Metabolic acidosis would show low pH and low HCO₃⁻ with low PaCO₂ as compensation. This patient has a near-normal/slightly alkalotic pH.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PE is possible but would typically present acutely with symptoms. This ABG pattern is consistent with the normal physiologic changes of pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pre-eclampsia with renal failure would present with hypertension, proteinuria, and potentially metabolic acidosis, not a compensated respiratory alkalosis.

questions.stem: A patient presents with respiratory alkalosis. Which of the following conditions can cause respiratory alkalosis with normal PaO₂?

explanation.summary: Panic disorder causes hyperventilation from CNS-driven anxiety, not from hypoxemia. PaO₂ remains normal because the lungs are healthy. This is a non-hypoxemic cause of respiratory alkalosis, in contrast to pneumonia, ARDS, altitude, and CHF, which cause hypoxemic hyperventilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In panic disorder, the hyperventilation is driven by CNS stimulation (anxiety), not by low oxygen. The lungs function normally, so PaO₂ is normal or even elevated from the hyperventilation itself.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pneumonia impairs gas exchange, causing hypoxemia that triggers reflex hyperventilation. PaO₂ is typically decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ARDS causes severe V/Q mismatch and hypoxemia, which drives hyperventilation. PaO₂ is markedly decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.D: High altitude reduces inspired PO₂, causing hypoxemia and reflex hyperventilation through peripheral chemoreceptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CHF can cause pulmonary edema and V/Q mismatch, leading to hypoxemia-driven hyperventilation.

questions.stem: Aspirin (salicylate) overdose is a unique cause of respiratory alkalosis. In early salicylate toxicity, which of the following acid-base patterns is expected?

explanation.summary: In early salicylate overdose, aspirin directly stimulates the medullary respiratory center, causing hyperventilation and isolated respiratory alkalosis. As toxicity progresses, lactic acid accumulation from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation adds metabolic acidosis, creating a mixed picture.
explanation.whyCorrect: Salicylates stimulate the brainstem respiratory centers, causing early hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis. This is a non-hypoxemic cause. Later, the metabolic acidosis from lactate and salicylate itself creates a mixed respiratory alkalosis-metabolic acidosis pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metabolic acidosis develops later in salicylate toxicity from lactic acid and the salicylate anion. Early toxicity presents with isolated respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Salicylates stimulate (not depress) the respiratory center, causing hyperventilation. Respiratory acidosis would require respiratory depression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Metabolic alkalosis is not caused by salicylate overdose. The metabolic component is acidosis from lactate accumulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis is not the typical pattern. Salicylates cause respiratory alkalosis (from CNS stimulation) combined with metabolic acidosis (from lactic acid) in later stages.

questions.stem: Cerebral vasoconstriction is a neurological consequence of respiratory alkalosis. This vasoconstriction is caused by:

explanation.summary: CO₂ is a potent cerebral vasodilator. In respiratory alkalosis, decreased PaCO₂ (hypocapnia) removes this vasodilatory stimulus, causing cerebral vasoconstriction. This reduces cerebral blood flow, contributing to dizziness, lightheadedness, and syncope.
explanation.whyCorrect: Low PaCO₂ causes cerebral vasoconstriction because CO₂ normally dilates cerebral vessels. Hypocapnia reduces this dilation, decreasing cerebral blood flow and causing neurological symptoms like dizziness, lightheadedness, and visual changes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased PaCO₂ causes cerebral vasodilation, not constriction. Hypercapnia (as in respiratory acidosis) increases cerebral blood flow.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ionized calcium decreases (not increases) in alkalemia. Decreased ionized calcium causes paresthesias and neuromuscular excitability, not cerebral vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alkalemia causes hypokalemia (K⁺ shifts into cells), not hyperkalemia. Hypokalemia is not the mechanism of cerebral vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated bicarbonate would be seen in metabolic alkalosis or compensated respiratory acidosis. In respiratory alkalosis, bicarbonate decreases with compensation.

questions.stem: Which ABG pattern is consistent with chronic respiratory alkalosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: pH slightly ↑ or normal, PaCO₂ ↓, HCO₃⁻ ↓

explanation.summary: In chronic respiratory alkalosis, sustained hyperventilation lowers PaCO₂. The kidneys compensate over days by excreting bicarbonate, lowering HCO₃⁻. The pH is slightly elevated or near-normal due to partial compensation. Pattern: pH slightly ↑/normal, PaCO₂ ↓, HCO₃⁻ ↓.
explanation.whyCorrect: The chronic nature allows renal compensation (bicarbonate excretion) to lower HCO₃⁻ and bring pH closer to normal. The pattern of low PaCO₂ with low HCO₃⁻ and near-normal pH distinguishes chronic from acute respiratory alkalosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Normal HCO₃⁻ with low PaCO₂ and elevated pH describes acute respiratory alkalosis, where the kidneys have not had time to compensate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: pH ↓ with PaCO₂ ↑ and HCO₃⁻ ↑ describes chronic respiratory acidosis, the opposite disturbance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: pH ↑ with PaCO₂ ↑ and HCO₃⁻ ↑ could suggest metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation. It is not respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: pH ↓ with both PaCO₂ ↓ and HCO₃⁻ ↓ describes metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (hyperventilation to blow off CO₂).

questions.stem: A patient with sudden onset respiratory alkalosis, pleuritic chest pain, and unilateral leg swelling should be evaluated for which potentially fatal condition?

explanation.summary: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a potentially fatal cause of respiratory alkalosis that must always be considered in unexplained hyperventilation. The triad of sudden dyspnea/tachypnea, pleuritic chest pain, and signs of deep venous thrombosis (unilateral leg swelling) strongly suggests PE.
explanation.whyCorrect: PE causes V/Q mismatch and often hypoxemia, triggering reflex hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis. The combination of acute respiratory alkalosis, pleuritic chest pain, and leg swelling (suggestive of DVT) should prompt urgent evaluation with D-dimer and CT angiography.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Panic disorder causes respiratory alkalosis but does not present with pleuritic chest pain and unilateral leg swelling. These signs suggest a thromboembolic process.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Asthma exacerbation causes wheezing and dyspnea from bronchoconstriction. It does not typically present with pleuritic chest pain and leg swelling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pneumothorax causes sudden dyspnea and chest pain but presents with absent breath sounds, not leg swelling suggestive of DVT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GERD causes heartburn and chest discomfort but does not cause respiratory alkalosis, pleuritic pain, or leg swelling.

questions.stem: The management of respiratory alkalosis primarily involves:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Identifying and treating the underlying cause

choices.label: C
choices.text: Breathing into a paper bag for all patients

explanation.summary: The treatment of respiratory alkalosis focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause. Respiratory alkalosis itself is more of a symptom than a primary disease. If hypoxemia is present, oxygen should be administered while the cause is investigated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Respiratory alkalosis is driven by hyperventilation, which has diverse causes (anxiety, PE, pneumonia, sepsis, pregnancy). Treatment targets the underlying condition: anxiolytics for panic, anticoagulation for PE, antibiotics for pneumonia, etc. The alkalosis resolves when the cause is treated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IV bicarbonate would worsen alkalosis by adding more base. It is used for metabolic acidosis, not respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Breathing into a paper bag is an outdated practice that can be dangerous (especially if the cause is PE or pneumonia). It should not be recommended for all patients with respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhaled CO₂ is not a standard treatment. While it would theoretically raise PaCO₂, addressing the underlying cause is the appropriate management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While hypokalemia can occur in alkalosis, potassium supplements alone do not treat the underlying cause of respiratory alkalosis.

questions.stem: Respiratory alkalosis has only one direct pathophysiologic cause, which is:

explanation.summary: Respiratory alkalosis is caused exclusively by increased alveolar ventilation (hyperventilation), which leads to excessive CO₂ excretion and reduced PaCO₂. The numerous etiologies (anxiety, PE, sepsis, altitude) all converge on this single mechanism — excessive CO₂ loss from the lungs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Every cause of respiratory alkalosis — whether hypoxemia-driven (altitude, pneumonia) or non-hypoxemic (panic, sepsis, pregnancy) — produces the alkalosis through the same final pathway: increased alveolar ventilation causing excessive CO₂ elimination.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased metabolic acid production leads to metabolic acidosis, not respiratory alkalosis. The respiratory system may compensate for metabolic acidosis through hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Renal bicarbonate retention is the compensation for respiratory acidosis, not a cause of respiratory alkalosis. In respiratory alkalosis, the kidneys excrete bicarbonate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased cardiac output does not directly cause respiratory alkalosis. If anything, it may lead to tissue hypoxia and compensatory hyperventilation, but the direct cause remains increased ventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Impaired gas exchange typically causes hypoxemia, which can trigger hyperventilation. But the direct cause of the alkalosis is the resulting increased ventilation, not the gas exchange impairment itself.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with heart failure presents with crackles, JVD, and lower extremity edema. His ABG shows: pH 7.55, PaCO₂ 25 mmHg, PaO₂ 70 mmHg. The respiratory alkalosis in this patient is most likely caused by:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypoxemia from pulmonary edema triggering reflex hyperventilation

explanation.summary: Heart failure causes pulmonary edema, which impairs gas exchange and produces hypoxemia (PaO₂ 70 mmHg). The hypoxemia stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors, triggering reflex hyperventilation that causes respiratory alkalosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pulmonary edema from heart failure creates V/Q mismatch and diffusion impairment, reducing PaO₂. Peripheral chemoreceptors detect the hypoxemia and increase ventilatory drive, causing excessive CO₂ elimination and respiratory alkalosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While anxiety can cause hyperventilation, this patient's clinical picture (crackles, JVD, edema, hypoxemia) clearly points to heart failure with hypoxemia-driven hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Metabolic compensation involves the kidneys adjusting bicarbonate, not changing ventilation. The hyperventilation here is a primary respiratory response to hypoxemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Opioid use would cause respiratory depression and acidosis, not alkalosis with hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The patient is in a clinical setting with heart failure symptoms. There is no indication of high altitude exposure.

questions.stem: A patient with sepsis has a respiratory rate of 30 breaths/min. The ABG shows: pH 7.48, PaCO₂ 30 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 22 mEq/L. Sepsis causes respiratory alkalosis through which mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct bacterial invasion of the respiratory center

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cytokine-mediated stimulation of the respiratory center

explanation.summary: Sepsis causes respiratory alkalosis through inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) that directly stimulate the brainstem respiratory centers, increasing ventilatory drive beyond CO₂ production and causing hypocapnia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inflammatory mediators released during sepsis stimulate the medullary respiratory centers, increasing ventilatory drive. The resulting hyperventilation exceeds CO₂ production, causing respiratory alkalosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bacteria do not typically directly invade the respiratory center. The stimulation is mediated by inflammatory cytokines from the systemic immune response.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sepsis can cause ARDS, but the primary mechanism of respiratory alkalosis is CNS stimulation by cytokines, not alveolar destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Renal bicarbonate retention is the compensation for respiratory acidosis, not a cause of respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchospasm causes airway obstruction, not respiratory alkalosis. It is more associated with obstructive diseases like asthma.

questions.stem: A hyperventilating patient has severe respiratory alkalosis. Which electrolyte disturbance is most likely?

explanation.summary: In alkalemia, H⁺ shifts out of cells into the blood to buffer the elevated pH. K⁺ moves into cells to maintain electrical neutrality, causing hypokalemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alkalemia promotes the exchange of intracellular K⁺ for extracellular H⁺. Potassium shifts into cells, lowering serum K⁺ levels and potentially causing muscle weakness, arrhythmias, and other hypokalemic symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alkalemia causes hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia. K⁺ shifts into cells in exchange for H⁺.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sodium levels are not significantly affected by acute respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Total calcium may be unchanged, but ionized calcium decreases. Total hypercalcemia does not occur.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Phosphate levels are not typically elevated in respiratory alkalosis.

questions.stem: Kussmaul respirations can be confused with hyperventilation from respiratory alkalosis. The ABG distinguishes them because Kussmaul breathing shows:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low pH with low HCO₃⁻ and compensatory low PaCO₂

explanation.summary: Kussmaul respirations are deep, labored breathing that compensates for severe metabolic acidosis (e.g., DKA). The ABG shows low pH, low HCO₃⁻ (primary metabolic disturbance), and low PaCO₂ (compensatory hyperventilation) — distinguishing it from primary respiratory alkalosis which shows elevated pH.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Kussmaul breathing, the ABG shows: pH < 7.35, low HCO₃⁻ (the primary metabolic problem), and low PaCO₂ (respiratory compensation). In primary respiratory alkalosis, pH > 7.45 with low PaCO₂ as the primary disturbance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Elevated pH with low PaCO₂ describes primary respiratory alkalosis, not Kussmaul breathing from metabolic acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated PaCO₂ with low pH describes respiratory acidosis. Kussmaul breathing shows low PaCO₂ from compensatory hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Normal values would indicate no acid-base disturbance. Kussmaul breathing is a response to significant metabolic acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated HCO₃⁻ with elevated pH describes metabolic alkalosis, not the acidosis that triggers Kussmaul breathing.

questions.stem: Respiratory alkalosis from cirrhosis is thought to be caused by:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Altered serum biochemistry (elevated progesterone and ammonia) stimulating the respiratory center

explanation.summary: Cirrhosis causes chronic respiratory alkalosis through accumulation of substances that stimulate the brainstem respiratory center, including progesterone (normally metabolized by the liver) and ammonia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatic dysfunction leads to impaired metabolism of progesterone and ammonia. These accumulated substances stimulate the medullary respiratory centers, causing chronic hyperventilation independent of hypoxemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ascites and portal hypertension can restrict diaphragm movement but would impair ventilation, not stimulate hyperventilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The liver does not produce excess CO₂ in cirrhosis. The alkalosis is from increased ventilation driven by chemical stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepatorenal syndrome causes metabolic acidosis from renal failure, not respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ascitic fluid restriction would impair ventilation, potentially causing hypoventilation, not hyperventilation.

questions.stem: In respiratory alkalosis, the decrease in CO₂ shifts the bicarbonate buffer equilibrium leftward. As a result, which of the following occurs?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Only H⁺ changes; HCO₃⁻ is unaffected

explanation.summary: When PaCO₂ decreases, the equilibrium shifts left: H⁺ and HCO₃⁻ combine to form H₂CO₃ → CO₂ + H₂O. Both H⁺ and HCO₃⁻ are consumed in this process. The decreased H⁺ raises pH; the decreased HCO₃⁻ is the initial chemical (non-renal) bicarbonate reduction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The leftward shift consumes both H⁺ and HCO₃⁻ as they recombine. This explains why pH rises (less H⁺) and HCO₃⁻ drops slightly even before renal compensation begins.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both are consumed in the leftward shift, so both decrease, not increase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: H⁺ decreases (not increases) because it is consumed. This is why pH rises.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While H⁺ decreases (correct), HCO₃⁻ also decreases because it too is consumed in the reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both H⁺ and HCO₃⁻ change. They are both consumed in the leftward equilibrium shift.

questions.stem: A patient with a pulmonary embolism develops respiratory alkalosis. Unlike most causes, PE can present with hyperventilation either with or without hypoxemia. Which initial test should be performed when PE is suspected in a patient with unexplained respiratory alkalosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: D-dimer followed by CT angiography if positive

explanation.summary: When PE is suspected, the initial workup involves D-dimer testing (a sensitive but nonspecific marker of thrombosis). If elevated, CT angiography (CTA) of the chest is performed to visualize thrombus in the pulmonary arteries and confirm the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: D-dimer has high sensitivity for thromboembolism. A negative D-dimer essentially rules out PE in low-risk patients. If positive, CT angiography provides definitive visualization of pulmonary arterial thrombi.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Echocardiography may show right heart strain in massive PE but is not the initial diagnostic test for suspected PE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sputum culture diagnoses pulmonary infections, not pulmonary embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bronchoscopy evaluates the airways and is not used to diagnose PE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spirometry measures lung function (FEV₁, FVC) and does not diagnose thromboembolic disease.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with hyperthyroidism and an ABG showing respiratory alkalosis. The hyperventilation in hyperthyroidism is driven by:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased metabolic demand and CO₂ production stimulating ventilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct thyroid hormone effect on the diaphragm

explanation.summary: Hyperthyroidism increases basal metabolic rate, which increases O₂ consumption and CO₂ production. The body responds by increasing ventilation to match the higher CO₂ output. If ventilation exceeds the increased CO₂ production, respiratory alkalosis results.
explanation.whyCorrect: Elevated thyroid hormones increase cellular metabolism, raising CO₂ production. The respiratory center increases ventilation to match. If the ventilatory response overshoots the increased metabolic demand, PaCO₂ falls below normal, producing respiratory alkalosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hyperthyroidism causes hyperthermia, not hypothalamic cooling. The hyperventilation is from increased metabolic demand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thyroid hormones do not directly act on the diaphragm to cause hyperventilation. The effect is indirect through increased metabolic demand.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thyroid hormones do not inhibit carbonic anhydrase. The hyperventilation is driven by increased CO₂ production from heightened metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While a large goiter can compress the trachea, this would cause stridor or upper airway obstruction, not respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common overall cause of respiratory alkalosis?

explanation.summary: Panic disorder (anxiety) is the most common overall cause of respiratory alkalosis. It triggers hyperventilation through CNS stimulation without any underlying pulmonary pathology.
explanation.whyCorrect: Panic disorder produces hyperventilation from anxiety-driven stimulation of the respiratory center. It is extremely common, affecting millions of people, and is the single most frequent cause of respiratory alkalosis in clinical practice.

whyWrongByChoice.A: High altitude is a cause of hypoxemic respiratory alkalosis but affects a much smaller population than panic disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pneumonia causes hypoxemia-driven hyperventilation but is less common than anxiety as a cause of respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PE is an important but relatively uncommon cause of respiratory alkalosis compared to anxiety.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sepsis causes respiratory alkalosis through cytokine stimulation but is less common than panic disorder.

questions.stem: A patient with respiratory alkalosis and hypoxemia should receive which immediate intervention?

explanation.summary: All hypoxic patients with respiratory alkalosis should receive immediate supplemental oxygen while the underlying cause is investigated. Oxygen addresses the life-threatening hypoxemia; the underlying cause (PE, pneumonia, CHF) requires specific treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: When respiratory alkalosis is accompanied by hypoxemia, the hyperventilation is a compensatory response to low oxygen. The priority is correcting hypoxemia with supplemental O₂ while pursuing the diagnosis and treating the underlying condition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IV bicarbonate would worsen the alkalosis and is not indicated in respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anxiolytics are appropriate for anxiety-induced hyperventilation with normal oxygenation, not for hypoxemic respiratory alkalosis where a serious underlying cause must be treated.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intubation is reserved for respiratory failure. Hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis typically responds to supplemental oxygen and treatment of the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A rebreathing mask would increase CO₂ but is dangerous in a hypoxemic patient. It could worsen hypoxemia by reducing inspired O₂.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man is found comatose at the scene of a house fire. He has singed nasal hair and cherry-red skin. Pulse oximetry reads 98%. Which of the following best explains the normal pulse oximetry reading?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carboxyhemoglobin absorbs light similarly to oxyhemoglobin, falsely elevating SpO₂

choices.label: C
choices.text: CO increases dissolved oxygen in plasma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Burns cause peripheral vasodilation improving oximetry readings

explanation.summary: Standard pulse oximeters use two wavelengths to distinguish oxyhemoglobin from deoxyhemoglobin. Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) has a similar light absorption spectrum to oxyhemoglobin at these wavelengths, so the device reads COHb as oxygenated hemoglobin, giving a falsely normal SpO₂.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pulse oximetry cannot differentiate COHb from O₂Hb because both absorb light at 660 nm similarly. Despite significant CO poisoning and tissue hypoxia, SpO₂ appears normal. CO-oximetry (measuring multiple wavelengths) or arterial blood gas with co-oximetry is needed for accurate diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The patient is severely hypoxic due to CO displacing O₂ from hemoglobin. The normal SpO₂ is falsely reassuring, not an indication of adequate oxygenation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CO does not increase dissolved oxygen. CO binds hemoglobin and also inhibits O₂ release at tissues, worsening hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fire exposure does not increase hemoglobin concentration. CO reduces the functional oxygen-carrying capacity of existing hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Burns may cause peripheral edema, but this does not explain the falsely normal pulse oximetry. The issue is spectrophotometric, not circulatory.

questions.stem: Carbon monoxide binds hemoglobin with approximately 240 times greater affinity than oxygen. In addition to displacing O₂, CO causes tissue hypoxia through which additional mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing the P50 and promoting O₂ unloading

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left-shifting the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve, impairing O₂ release

explanation.summary: Beyond occupying O₂-binding sites, CO allosterically increases the O₂ affinity of the remaining functional heme groups, shifting the dissociation curve left. This impairs O₂ release at the tissues, making CO poisoning worse than an equivalent degree of anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: CO binding to one heme site increases the remaining sites' affinity for O₂ (left shift, decreased P50). This means whatever O₂ is bound is held more tightly and released less readily, compounding the reduced carrying capacity with impaired delivery.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing P50 would shift the curve right and promote O₂ unloading, which would be beneficial. CO does the opposite — it decreases P50 (left shift).
whyWrongByChoice.C: CO does not cause hemolysis. It binds to hemoglobin reversibly. The red blood cells remain intact; it is their functional capacity that is impaired.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CO does not block ventilation at the larynx. It is an inhaled gas that enters the blood through the alveoli and binds hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CO does not inactivate carbonic anhydrase. CO binds heme-containing proteins (hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochrome c oxidase), not zinc-containing enzymes like carbonic anhydrase.

questions.stem: Cyanide poisoning causes cellular hypoxia through which primary mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Binding hemoglobin and preventing oxygen transport

choices.label: B
choices.text: Destroying alveolar epithelium and impairing gas exchange

choices.label: C
choices.text: Irreversibly inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase, blocking the electron transport chain

explanation.summary: Cyanide binds to ferric iron (Fe³⁺) in mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV), blocking the electron transport chain and preventing cells from using oxygen for aerobic metabolism. This causes severe lactic acidosis from forced anaerobic metabolism and can rapidly lead to cell death.
explanation.whyCorrect: CN⁻ binds the Fe³⁺ in cytochrome c oxidase, the terminal enzyme in the mitochondrial electron transport chain. This halts oxidative phosphorylation, preventing ATP production despite adequate oxygen delivery. Cells cannot use available O₂, leading to histotoxic hypoxia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Binding hemoglobin is the mechanism of CO poisoning, not cyanide. Cyanide acts intracellularly at the mitochondria, not on hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyanide does not primarily destroy alveolar epithelium. Its toxicity is at the cellular mitochondrial level, not the gas exchange surface.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cyanide does not cause bronchospasm. Its mechanism is inhibition of cellular respiration at the mitochondrial level.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cyanide does not deplete surfactant. The pathophysiology is intracellular — inhibition of the electron transport chain.

questions.stem: A fire victim presents with severe lactic acidosis (lactate >8 mM), altered mental status, and mydriasis. These findings are most consistent with poisoning by which agent?

explanation.summary: Severe lactic acidosis (>8 mM), altered mental status, and mydriasis (pupil dilation) in a fire victim strongly suggest cyanide poisoning. CN inhibits cytochrome c oxidase, forcing anaerobic metabolism with severe lactate production. Mydriasis is a noted diagnostic symptom specific to CN toxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cyanide produces severe lactic acidosis (typically >8 mM) from blocked aerobic metabolism. Mydriasis is a characteristic finding that helps distinguish CN from CO poisoning (which can cause mild lactic acidosis ~3 mM and does not typically cause mydriasis).

whyWrongByChoice.A: CO poisoning can cause mild lactic acidosis (~3 mM) and neurological symptoms, but severe lactic acidosis (>8 mM) with mydriasis is more consistent with cyanide.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methanol poisoning causes metabolic acidosis with an osmolar gap and can cause visual symptoms, but it is not associated with fire exposure or mydriasis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ethylene glycol causes metabolic acidosis with oxalate crystals in urine. It is not associated with fire exposure or mydriasis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetaminophen overdose causes hepatotoxicity. It does not present with mydriasis and severe lactic acidosis in the context of fire exposure.

questions.stem: The most common source of combined CO and CN poisoning is:

explanation.summary: Smoke inhalation during fires is the most common source of combined CO and CN poisoning. CO is produced by incomplete combustion of carbon-containing materials, while CN (hydrogen cyanide gas) is released from burning synthetic materials like plastics, polyurethane, nylon, and silk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fires produce both CO (from incomplete combustion of organic materials) and HCN (from combustion of nitrogen-containing synthetics). An estimated 35-40% of fire victims have toxic cyanide levels, and smoke inhalation accounts for 75-80% of fire-related deaths.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Automobile exhaust is a common source of CO alone but does not typically produce cyanide.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Faulty heating systems produce CO from incomplete combustion but do not generate cyanide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Industrial exposure can cause either CO or CN poisoning separately, but combined exposure is most common in fires.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Medication overdose (e.g., nitroprusside) can cause CN toxicity but does not simultaneously produce CO poisoning.

questions.stem: The immediate treatment for CO poisoning involves administering 100% oxygen. This treatment works by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Directly neutralizing CO in the blood

choices.label: B
choices.text: Competitively displacing CO from hemoglobin and shortening its half-life

choices.label: E
choices.text: Converting CO to CO₂ for elimination

explanation.summary: High-concentration oxygen (100% FiO₂) increases the PaO₂, which competitively displaces CO from hemoglobin binding sites. This reduces the half-life of COHb from approximately 5 hours on room air to approximately 1.5 hours on 100% O₂, and even shorter with hyperbaric oxygen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Since CO and O₂ compete for the same hemoglobin binding sites, dramatically increasing PaO₂ with 100% oxygen shifts the equilibrium toward O₂ binding. This displaces CO, reduces COHb levels, and restores oxygen-carrying capacity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oxygen does not chemically neutralize CO. It works by competitive displacement from hemoglobin binding sites.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 100% oxygen does not stimulate erythropoiesis. Its benefit is immediate — displacing CO from existing hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CO is not metabolized by the liver. It is eliminated primarily through the lungs by competitive displacement with oxygen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CO is not converted to CO₂ in the body. CO is exhaled unchanged once displaced from hemoglobin.

questions.stem: The definitive antidote for cyanide poisoning is hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit). Its mechanism of action involves:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Competitively displacing CN from cytochrome c oxidase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Binding CN to form cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), which is renally excreted

choices.label: E
choices.text: Converting CN to CO for pulmonary exhalation

explanation.summary: Hydroxocobalamin directly binds free cyanide ions in the blood to form cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), which is non-toxic and excreted by the kidneys. This reduces the amount of free CN available to inhibit cytochrome c oxidase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hydroxocobalamin has a cobalt center that has high affinity for CN⁻. It chelates free cyanide, forming cyanocobalamin (a form of vitamin B12) that is safely eliminated in the urine. This is the preferred first-line antidote for CN poisoning.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hydroxocobalamin does not directly displace CN from cytochrome c oxidase. It works by scavenging free CN in the blood before it reaches the mitochondria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepatic metabolism of cyanide (via rhodanese and thiosulfate) is the body's natural detoxification pathway but is slow. Hydroxocobalamin works by direct binding, not by enhancing hepatic metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydroxocobalamin does not stimulate the electron transport chain. It reduces CN levels so that cytochrome c oxidase can resume normal function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CN is not converted to CO. Hydroxocobalamin converts CN to cyanocobalamin, not carbon monoxide.

questions.stem: A key diagnostic test for confirming CO poisoning is measurement of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) levels. Which method is used for this measurement?

explanation.summary: CO-oximetry uses multiple wavelengths of light to specifically distinguish COHb from OxyHb and other hemoglobin species. It is performed on an arterial blood sample and provides the definitive measurement of COHb levels for diagnosing CO poisoning.
explanation.whyCorrect: CO-oximetry uses 4+ wavelengths (vs 2 for standard pulse ox) to differentiate oxyhemoglobin, deoxyhemoglobin, carboxyhemoglobin, and methemoglobin. A COHb level >3% (non-smoker) or >10% (smoker) confirms significant CO exposure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Standard pulse oximetry uses only two wavelengths and cannot distinguish COHb from OxyHb, giving falsely normal readings in CO poisoning.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CBC measures hemoglobin concentration and blood cell counts but cannot detect COHb or diagnose CO poisoning.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serum lactate may be elevated in CO poisoning but is nonspecific. It cannot confirm the diagnosis or quantify CO exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chest X-ray evaluates lung structure but cannot measure COHb levels or diagnose CO poisoning.

questions.stem: CO poisoning can produce delayed neuropsychiatric sequelae days to weeks after the initial exposure. Which of the following is a recognized delayed complication?

explanation.summary: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) can develop days to weeks after CO poisoning, even after apparent clinical recovery. Manifestations include cognitive deficits, memory impairment, personality changes, and movement disorders from delayed neuronal injury and demyelination.
explanation.whyCorrect: DNS occurs in up to 30-40% of severe CO poisoning survivors. The delayed damage is thought to result from oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, and immune-mediated demyelination in the brain triggered during the initial poisoning episode.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CO poisoning can cause acute pulmonary edema but is not typically associated with delayed pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic kidney disease is not a recognized delayed complication of CO poisoning. Acute kidney injury may occur from rhabdomyolysis in severe cases but chronic disease is uncommon.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepatic cirrhosis is not caused by CO poisoning. Acute hepatic injury can occur in severe poisoning but chronic cirrhosis does not develop.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bone marrow failure is not a recognized complication of CO poisoning.

questions.stem: Sodium nitroprusside is an IV vasodilator used for hypertensive emergencies. Prolonged use can cause cyanide toxicity because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nitroprusside contains cyanide groups that are released during metabolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nitroprusside blocks cytochrome c oxidase directly

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nitroprusside is converted to CO in the liver

explanation.summary: Sodium nitroprusside contains five cyanide groups per molecule. During metabolism, these CN groups are released and must be detoxified by hepatic rhodanese (which converts CN to thiocyanate using thiosulfate). Prolonged use or rapid infusion can overwhelm this detoxification pathway, causing cyanide accumulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The nitroprusside molecule (Na₂[Fe(CN)₅NO]) contains five CN⁻ groups that are liberated during drug metabolism. If the rate of CN release exceeds the capacity of rhodanese to convert it to thiocyanate, toxic levels accumulate, inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Nitroprusside does not directly block cytochrome c oxidase. The released cyanide from its metabolism is what inhibits this enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitroprusside is not converted to CO. It releases cyanide ions, not carbon monoxide, during metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroprusside does not deplete hemoglobin. Its toxicity is related to cyanide release, not hemoglobin effects.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While nitroprusside can theoretically cause mild methemoglobinemia through its nitric oxide component, the clinically significant toxicity is from cyanide release, not methemoglobin formation.

questions.stem: CO poisoning has different effects on oxygen delivery compared to anemia. If a patient with CO poisoning and a patient with anemia both have a 50% reduction in functional hemoglobin, the CO-poisoned patient will have MORE tissue hypoxia because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: CO destroys red blood cells, while anemia does not

choices.label: B
choices.text: CO additionally left-shifts the dissociation curve, impairing O₂ release from remaining functional hemoglobin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anemia patients have compensatory erythropoiesis that CO patients lack

choices.label: D
choices.text: CO patients have lower plasma oxygen levels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anemia increases cardiac output more effectively than CO poisoning

explanation.summary: With the same 50% reduction in O₂-carrying capacity, CO poisoning is worse than anemia because CO also left-shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve. In anemia, the remaining hemoglobin releases O₂ normally. In CO poisoning, the remaining O₂-bound hemoglobin holds O₂ more tightly, compounding the delivery deficit.
explanation.whyCorrect: CO has a dual mechanism: (1) it occupies binding sites, reducing carrying capacity (like anemia), and (2) it allosterically increases O₂ affinity of the remaining functional sites (left shift). This means the remaining O₂ is released less readily at tissues, making CO poisoning more dangerous than equivalent anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CO does not destroy red blood cells. It reversibly binds hemoglobin. The RBCs are intact in both conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Compensatory erythropoiesis takes weeks and is not relevant to acute CO poisoning. The immediate difference is the left shift of the dissociation curve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasma oxygen levels (PaO₂) are actually normal in CO poisoning because PaO₂ reflects dissolved O₂, which is unaffected by CO. The difference is in the curve shift.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both conditions can trigger compensatory increases in cardiac output. The critical difference is the left shift that prevents O₂ release in CO poisoning.

questions.stem: The half-life of carboxyhemoglobin on room air is approximately 5 hours. With 100% normobaric oxygen, it decreases to approximately:

explanation.summary: 100% normobaric oxygen reduces COHb half-life from ~5 hours to ~1.5 hours by massively increasing PaO₂ and competitively displacing CO from hemoglobin. Hyperbaric oxygen further reduces it to ~20-30 minutes.
explanation.whyCorrect: 100% O₂ increases PaO₂ dramatically, enhancing competitive displacement of CO. The ~1.5 hour half-life represents a significant improvement and is the basis for immediate high-flow O₂ therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 4 hours represents only minimal improvement over the 5-hour room air half-life.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 3 hours underestimates the benefit of 100% O₂. The accepted half-life is approximately 1.5 hours.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 30 minutes is closer to the half-life with hyperbaric oxygen, not normobaric 100% O₂.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 5 minutes is too rapid even for hyperbaric oxygen.

questions.stem: CO poisoning peaks during which season?

explanation.summary: CO poisoning peaks in winter due to increased indoor heater use. Faulty or poorly ventilated heating systems in enclosed spaces are the most common non-fire source of CO exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Winter brings increased use of gas furnaces, space heaters, wood stoves, and fireplaces. Faulty equipment in sealed homes allows CO to accumulate. Fifty-seven percent of CO-related deaths are linked to automobile exhaust, also more common in enclosed garages during cold months.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Summer does not see peak CO poisoning. Indoor heating use is minimal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Spring does not have seasonal CO poisoning risk factors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fall has some early heating use but does not represent the peak.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CO poisoning clearly peaks in winter months.
